labels.name: Parent class
labels.desc: The class being extended — defines shared structure and default behavior

labels.name: Child class
labels.desc: A class that inherits from another and can provide its own version of inherited behavior

labels.name: Override
labels.desc: A child redefining a parent method with its own implementation

labels.name: Polymorphism
labels.desc: Different objects responding to the same method call in their own way

labels.name: Abstract
labels.desc: A method or class that must be completed by a subclass — cannot be used directly

labels.name: Super
labels.desc: Calling or referencing the parent class from inside the child

labels.name: Downcast
labels.desc: Treating a parent-typed reference as its specific child type after confirming what it actually is

prompts.title: Asset Value Hierarchy
prompts.statement: A financial app needs to track two types of assets: houses and vehicles. Both share common info like a description, date acquired, and original cost — but each calculates its current value differently.

expectedTags.phrase: two types of assets: houses and vehicles
expectedTags.explanation: Houses and vehicles are the specific types that must each provide their own implementation — they are the concrete child classes of an abstract blueprint that cannot be used on its own.

expectedTags.phrase: Both share common info like a description, date acquired, and original cost
expectedTags.explanation: The shared fields belong to a single blueprint that both types build on — that shared structure is what the parent class defines.

expectedTags.phrase: each calculates its current value differently
expectedTags.explanation: Each type provides its own version of the same calculation — that is override in action, and calling that calculation on a mixed list without knowing the type is polymorphism.

prompts.title: House Valuation
prompts.statement: A house's current value depends on its square footage and condition. Excellent condition drives the price per square foot up; poor condition drives it down. Lot size adds a fixed premium on top of the base calculation. A house builds on a shared asset record that includes the description and original cost.

expectedTags.phrase: A house builds on a shared asset record that includes the description and original cost
expectedTags.explanation: The house type doesn't define the description and original cost itself — it passes those up to the shared record when it is created, which is what calling super in the constructor does.

expectedTags.phrase: A house's current value depends on its square footage and condition
expectedTags.explanation: This is the house's own version of the value calculation — a specific rule that only this type applies, overriding the placeholder defined in the shared blueprint.

expectedTags.phrase: Lot size adds a fixed premium on top of the base calculation
expectedTags.explanation: Adding a lot-size premium is part of what makes the house's calculation unique — it is logic that only lives in the house's version of getCurrentValue.

prompts.title: Vehicle Depreciation
prompts.statement: A vehicle's current value depends on its age and mileage. Depreciation increases in steps as the vehicle gets older, and high mileage reduces the final value — unless it's a Honda or Toyota, which hold their value better. A vehicle builds on a shared asset record that stores the original cost.

expectedTags.phrase: A vehicle builds on a shared asset record that stores the original cost
expectedTags.explanation: The vehicle type retrieves the original cost from the shared record rather than storing it separately — accessing that value through the parent is what super-based access makes possible.

expectedTags.phrase: Depreciation increases in steps as the vehicle gets older
expectedTags.explanation: This stepped depreciation is the vehicle's own version of the value calculation — specific logic that only this type provides, overriding the abstract requirement from the shared blueprint.

expectedTags.phrase: high mileage reduces the final value
expectedTags.explanation: Mileage is a field that belongs specifically to the Vehicle blueprint — it is part of the data that defines what a vehicle object knows about itself.

expectedTags.phrase: Honda or Toyota, which hold their value better
expectedTags.explanation: Even within the vehicle type, different makes produce different final values from the same calculation call — the same method produces a different result depending on the object's state.

prompts.title: Mixed Asset List
prompts.statement: The financial app stores both houses and vehicles in one collection. When displaying the portfolio, it should show each asset's description and current value — without needing to know which specific type each one is.

expectedTags.phrase: stores both houses and vehicles in one collection
expectedTags.explanation: Holding different specific types together in one collection is possible because both share a common parent type — and polymorphism is what lets you call the same method on each without knowing which type it is.

expectedTags.phrase: show each asset's description and current value
expectedTags.explanation: When getCurrentValue is called on each item, the correct version runs automatically — each specific type has its own override that produces the right result.

expectedTags.phrase: without needing to know which specific type each one is
expectedTags.explanation: This is the defining feature of polymorphism — the code that loops through the collection does not need to branch on type, because each object responds correctly to the same method call.

prompts.title: Type-Specific Asset Details
prompts.statement: After displaying the portfolio, the application needs to show extra details unique to each asset type — the address for a house, or the year and make for a vehicle. The app needs to figure out which type each asset is before accessing those details.

expectedTags.phrase: The app needs to figure out which type each asset is before accessing those details
expectedTags.explanation: When assets are stored under a shared type, the app must confirm what specific type each one actually is before it can access type-specific details — that confirmation and narrowing is what a downcast does.

expectedTags.phrase: extra details unique to each asset type
expectedTags.explanation: Fields like address or year and make exist only on the specific types, not on the shared record — they are defined in each child class and are only accessible after the type is confirmed.

expectedTags.phrase: the address for a house, or the year and make for a vehicle
expectedTags.explanation: Each specific type exposes its own details that the shared type does not know about — accessing them requires knowing which child type you are working with, which is the result of a successful downcast.

prompts.title: Shape Area Calculator
prompts.statement: A drawing app supports circles, rectangles, and triangles. All shapes need to report their area, but each calculates it differently. The app should be able to work with a mixed list of shapes and print each area without checking what type it is.

expectedTags.phrase: All shapes need to report their area
expectedTags.explanation: Requiring every shape to report an area — without providing the formula — is what an abstract method in a parent class does: it sets the expectation without fulfilling it.

expectedTags.phrase: each calculates it differently
expectedTags.explanation: Each specific shape type provides its own formula — circle, rectangle, and triangle are the child types that each override the area method with their own implementation.

expectedTags.phrase: without checking what type it is
expectedTags.explanation: The ability to call the same method on every shape in the list and get the right result — without branching on type — is polymorphism at work.

prompts.title: Checking and Savings Accounts
prompts.statement: A banking app has two account types: checking and savings. Both can accept deposits and process withdrawals the same way — but each calculates its monthly maintenance fee differently.

expectedTags.phrase: two account types: checking and savings
expectedTags.explanation: Checking and savings are the specific types that must each supply their own fee logic — they are concrete child classes of an abstract blueprint that defines the requirement without filling it in.

expectedTags.phrase: Both can accept deposits and process withdrawals the same way
expectedTags.explanation: Shared behavior that works identically for both types lives in the parent class — deposits and withdrawals are concrete methods defined once and available to all account types.

expectedTags.phrase: each calculates its monthly maintenance fee differently
expectedTags.explanation: Each account type provides its own version of the fee calculation — that is an override — and calling the same method on a mixed list of accounts and getting the right fee each time is polymorphism.

prompts.title: Warrior vs. Mage Damage
prompts.statement: In a game, warriors and mages are both characters — but they deal damage differently. A mage's damage calculation layers spell power on top of their base attack strength, while a warrior's relies solely on that base attack.

expectedTags.phrase: warriors and mages are both characters
expectedTags.explanation: "Characters" is the shared type that both warriors and mages belong to — it is the abstract parent that defines what every character must be able to do, while warrior and mage are the child types that fulfill that requirement.

expectedTags.phrase: they deal damage differently
expectedTags.explanation: Each type provides its own damage formula — that is an override — and the ability to call the same damage method on any character and get the right result is polymorphism.

expectedTags.phrase: layers spell power on top of their base attack strength
expectedTags.explanation: The mage's formula adds spell power to the base attack value that comes from the shared character record — accessing that inherited value from inside the mage's own method is what super enables.
